title: Indigo Resin
desc: The writing room is low on fresh marking resin, which means the Conjurors' notes are about to become harder to trust.

steps.text:
Every frightening discipline eventually reaches the same practical requirement: somebody has to label things. In the Conjurors' writing room, shelves of tablets, scroll tubes, and ceramic tags line the walls, all marked in a dark indigo resin that stains fingers for days and stone for longer. The room smells less ominous than the lower labs and more like crushed dye, warmed wax, and concentrated attention.

The record keeper sets out a mortar, a tray of indigo cakes, and a bundle of reed pens cut to different widths. "Grind fresh marking resin if you want the mixing bench," she says. "Or sort the reed pens and ceramic nibs if you prefer the tool shelf. The symbols may unsettle people. The supply work behind them is disappointingly ordinary."

options.label: Grind fresh indigo resin at the mixing bench
Sort the reed pens and ceramic nibs on the shelf

steps.text:
The indigo cakes start hard as stone and end as something between ink and lacquer after enough grinding, oil, and resin binder are worked through them. You crush the cakes to powder, fold in the binder, and keep the pestle moving until the mixture loses its grit and takes on the dense dark sheen the keeper wants. By the end, your hands are smudged blue at the knuckles and the whole bench looks like a storm cloud was processed there.

The keeper tests the finished resin on a scrap tablet with three sharp strokes. The lines hold clean, dark, and slightly glossy in the lamplight. That seems to satisfy her more than enthusiasm would have. "Good. Half the mystery in these tunnels is simply writing that refuses to fade when people start regretting it."

options.label: Seal the resin jar and leave the bench stocked

steps.text:
The pen shelf is arranged by purpose, not comfort. Broad reeds for wall marks, narrow points for archive slips, split nibs for the tiny annotation hands the senior Conjurors seem perversely fond of. Right now the whole selection has drifted into a messy truce with itself. You separate the reeds by cut width, match the ceramic nibs to their handles, and return the sharper points to the upper slots where fewer careless hands will find them.

By the time you finish, the shelf looks less like a tray of improvised tools and more like an instrument panel for ideas best approached cautiously. The keeper gives the top row a quick inspection, then nods. "Better. Precision is much easier to claim when the pens stop hiding from one another."

options.label: Brush the indigo dust from the shelf and step back
